questions.prompt_html:
Find equations of the three normal lines to the parabola given parametrically by the equations 
x(t) = t2, y(t) = 2t, which pass through the point P (3, 0).
questions.prompt_text: Find equations of the three normal lines to the parabola given parametrically by the equations x(t) = t 2 , y(t) = 2t, which pass through the point P (3, 0).
questions.solution_html:
Let Q (x, y) = (t2, 2t) be a point on the parabola such that PQ is a normal line.
The slope of PQ is given by m =  = .
On the other hand, the slope of a normal line is the negative reciprocal of the slope of the tangent line.
Hence m = -  = -  = -t.
Equating the two slopes, we obtain = - t 
It follows that 2t = - t (t2 - 3) or t3 - t = 0 ⇒ t (t - 1) (t + 1)= 0. 
The equation has three real roots: t = 0, 1, and -1
Therefore there are three normal lines.
If t =0, slope = -t = 0, the equation of the first normal line is y - 0 = 0 (x -3) or y = 0.
If t = 1, slope = -t = -1, the equation of the second normal line is y - 0 = -1 (x - 3) or y = 3 - x.
If t = -1, slope = - t = 1 the equation of the third normal line is y - 0 = 1 ( x - 3) or y = x -3.
The figure below shows the parabola and the three normal lines through the point (3, 0).
questions.solution_text: Let Q (x, y) = (t 2 , 2t) be a point on the parabola such that PQ is a normal line. The slope of PQ is given by m = (2t - 0/(t) with superscript (2) - 3) = (2t/(t) with superscript (2) - 3) . On the other hand, the slope of a normal line is the negative reciprocal of the slope of the tangent line. Hence m = - (dx/dt/dy/dt ) = - (2t/2) = -t. Equating the two slopes, we obtain (2t/(t) with superscript (2) - 3) = - t It follows that 2t = - t (t 2 - 3) or t 3 - t = 0 ⇒ t (t - 1) (t + 1)= 0. The equation has three real roots: t = 0, 1, and -1 Therefore there are three normal lines. If t =0, slope = -t = 0, the equation of the first normal line is y - 0 = 0 (x -3) or y = 0. If t = 1, slope = -t = -1, the equation of the second normal line is y - 0 = -1 (x - 3) or y = 3 - x. If t = -1, slope = - t = 1 the equation of the third normal line is y - 0 = 1 ( x - 3) or y = x -3. The figure below shows the parabola and the three normal lines through the point (3, 0). Short Description: A graph of a right opening parabola with vertex at the origin and focus at (3, 0). The dashed lines are drawn from (3, 0) to (1, 2) and (1, negative 2).

questions.prompt_html:
The equation of a conic section in polar coordinates is given by r = .
(i)Transform the equation of the conic section to rectangular coordinates (x, y).
(ii)Identify the conic section.
questions.prompt_text: The equation of a conic section in polar coordinates is given by r = (16/5 + 3cos(θ)) . (i) Transform the equation of the conic section to rectangular coordinates (x, y). (ii) Identify the conic section.
questions.solution_html:
(i) r =  ⇒ r(5 + 3cos(θ)) = 16 and hence 5r + 3r cos(θ) = 16 or 5r = 16 - 3rcos(θ)
Substituting x = rcos(θ) and r = , we obtain 
5 = 16 - 3x. Squaring both sides, we get 25(x2 + y2) = 256- 96x + 9x2.
Simplifying we obtain 16x2 + 25y2+ 96x = 256. Let us express in standard form:
Completing the square in the x-terms: 16(x + 3)2 + 25y2 = 400
Hence  +  = 1. 
(ii) The conic section is an ellipse.
questions.solution_text: (i) r = (16/5 + 3cos(θ)) ⇒ r(5 + 3cos(θ)) = 16 and hence 5r + 3r cos(θ) = 16 or 5r = 16 - 3rcos(θ) Substituting x = rcos(θ) and r = square root of ((x) with superscript (2) + (y) with superscript (2)) , we obtain 5 square root of ((x) with superscript (2) + (y) with superscript (2)) = 16 - 3x. Squaring both sides, we get 25(x 2 + y 2 ) = 256- 96x + 9x 2 . Simplifying we obtain 16x 2 + 25y 2 + 96x = 256. Let us express in standard form: Completing the square in the x-terms: 16(x + 3) 2 + 25y 2 = 400 Hence (((x +3)) with superscript (2)/25) + ((y) with superscript (2)/16) = 1. (ii) The conic section is an ellipse.

feedback_html.solution:
(i) r =  ⇒ r(5 + 3cos(θ)) = 16 and hence 5r + 3r cos(θ) = 16 or 5r = 16 - 3rcos(θ)
Substituting x = rcos(θ) and r = , we obtain 
5 = 16 - 3x. Squaring both sides, we get 25(x2 + y2) = 256- 96x + 9x2.
Simplifying we obtain 16x2 + 25y2+ 96x = 256. Let us express in standard form:
Completing the square in the x-terms: 16(x + 3)2 + 25y2 = 400
Hence  +  = 1. 
(ii) The conic section is an ellipse.

questions.prompt_html:
Sketch the graph of the conic section given by the polar equation r =   for ε = 0, 1, 0.7, and 2.
Identify each of the conic sections.
questions.prompt_text: Sketch the graph of the conic section given by the polar equation r = (2/1 -εcos(θ)) for ε = 0, 1, 0.7, and 2. Identify each of the conic sections.
questions.solution_html:
For ε = 0, the conic section is a circle (shown in blue)
For ε = 1, the conic section is a parabola (shown in red)  
For ε = 0.7, the conic section is an ellipse (shown in black)
For ε = 2, the conic section is a hyperbola (shown in dotted black)
questions.solution_text: Short Description: Graph of four functions. Long Description: The graphs are as follows. A right opening parabola with vertex at (negative 1, 0). The parabola passes through the points (0, 2) and (0, negative 2). A hyperbola with left branch passing through the point (negative 2, 0) and right branch passing through (negative 0.7, 0). An ellipse passing through the points (0, 2), (negative 1.2, 0), (0, negative 2), and (6.7, 0). A circle with center at (0, 0). The circle passes through the points (2, 0), (0, 2), (negative 2, 0), and (0, negative 2). For ε = 0, the conic section is a circle (shown in blue) For ε = 1, the conic section is a parabola (shown in red) For ε = 0.7, the conic section is an ellipse (shown in black) For ε = 2, the conic section is a hyperbola (shown in dotted black)

feedback_html.solution:
For ε = 0, the conic section is a circle (shown in blue)
For ε = 1, the conic section is a parabola (shown in red)  
For ε = 0.7, the conic section is an ellipse (shown in black)
For ε = 2, the conic section is a hyperbola (shown in dotted black)

images.alt: Short Description: Graph of four functions. Long Description: The graphs are as follows. A right opening parabola with vertex at (negative 1, 0). The parabola passes through the points (0, 2) and (0, negative 2). 	A hyperbola with left branch passing through the point (negative 2, 0) and right branch passing through (negative 0.7, 0). An ellipse passing through the points (0, 2), (negative 1.2, 0), (0, negative 2), and (6.7, 0). A circle with center at (0, 0). The circle passes through the points (2, 0), (0, 2), (negative 2, 0), and (0, negative 2).
